patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female project manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. nl_assertions: Explicitly states the diagnosis as insomnia using the word "insomnia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).
Explains that first-line treatment is CBT-I and briefly describes what it involves (communication milestone: explain_treatment).
States to avoid pentobarbital for insomnia (communication milestone: explain_treatment).
Assesses key insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, frequency and duration, and daytime impairment.

Screens for common contributors and alternative explanations: poor sleep hygiene, circadian rhythm issues, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, and medication-related insomnia (e.g., stimulants, steroids).
Screens for red flags/secondary sleep disorders that would change management: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness) and restless legs symptoms.

Does not prescribe any medication in an L1 task; if discussing future pharmacotherapy, clearly frames it as conditional and states that allergy, drug-interaction, and contraindication checks are required before prescribing (required safety checks).

Provides brief, practical sleep hygiene advice consistent with CBT-I principles (regular schedule, limit naps, avoid caffeine late, stimulus control), without presenting it as a replacement for CBT-I.
Offers a follow-up timeframe or criteria for reassessment (e.g., if symptoms persist/worsen, significant daytime impairment, or concern for OSA/RLS/mood disorder).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia and communicate first-line management with CBT-I while avoiding pentobarbital; no prescribing.

Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

Recommend CBT-I and basic sleep hygiene/stimulus control.
Advise avoiding pentobarbital.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct diagnosis documentation after appropriate evaluation; treatment actions are out of scope for required tool outcomes.

nl_assertions: States a diagnosis of insomnia explicitly using the term "insomnia".
Assesses core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, and/or non-restorative sleep with daytime impairment.
Clarifies duration and frequency (acute vs chronic pattern) and asks about sleep schedule and opportunity for sleep.
Screens for common contributors and mimics: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol use, shift work/irregular schedule, poor sleep hygiene, stress, pain, pregnancy/postpartum if relevant.
Screens for psychiatric comorbidity and safety: depression and anxiety symptoms, and asks about suicidal ideation when mood symptoms are present.

Screens for other sleep disorders when suggested by history: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs), circadian rhythm disorder (phase delay/advance), parasomnias.
Reviews medication/substance causes (stimulants, corticosteroids, decongestants, SSRIs/SNRIs activating effects, bupropion, thyroid hormone excess, illicit stimulants).
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly language and includes the word "insomnia".
Communication milestone: explains treatment options and includes both "CBT-I" and "sedative-hypnotics"; emphasizes CBT-I as first-line for chronic insomnia.

If discussing or considering prescribing sedative-hypnotics, performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (e.g., pregnancy, OSA/respiratory disease, substance use disorder risk, older adult fall risk, concurrent CNS depressants).

Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not advise alcohol for sleep; cautions against combining sedative-hypnotics with opioids/benzodiazepines/other CNS depressants; advises lowest effective dose and short duration if sedative-hypnotics are used.

Provides a basic follow-up recommendation (e.g., reassess symptoms and daytime function, adherence to CBT-I/sleep hygiene, and adverse effects if medications are used), without needing a follow-up tool action for L1.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after confirming sleep complaint with impairment and excluding obvious secondary causes; provide education on CBT-I and cautious discussion of sedative-hypnotics.
steps: Elicit insomnia symptom pattern and daytime consequences; confirm adequate sleep opportunity.
Characterize timeline (acute vs chronic) and precipitating/maintaining factors (stress, schedule).
Review substances and medications that can worsen sleep.
Screen for alternate sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder) and key medical/psychiatric contributors.
Explain diagnosis using the term insomnia and normalize commonality while validating distress.
Explain treatment: recommend CBT-I as first-line; discuss sleep hygiene and stimulus control; mention sedative-hypnotics as an option with risks and short-term use considerations.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant emphasizing nonpharmacologic management while still meeting communication milestones; medication discussion remains general without prescribing.
steps: Confirm insomnia symptoms, daytime impairment, and adequate sleep opportunity; determine duration.
Screen for OSA/RLS/circadian issues and review substances/medications.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and outline CBT-I components; briefly mention sedative-hypnotics as possible short-term adjunct with risks.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant that considers comorbid anxiety/depression and differentiates primary vs secondary insomnia; still records insomnia diagnosis and provides required explanations.
steps: Assess insomnia symptoms, impairment, and chronicity; ask about worry/rumination and mood symptoms.
Screen for suicidality if depressive symptoms are endorsed.
Review medications/substances and screen for OSA/RLS.
Explain insomnia and role of comorbid conditions; recommend CBT-I and discuss sedative-hypnotics in general terms.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and frames it in patient-friendly language.

Screens for key secondary causes and mimics: depression/anxiety symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication contributors, pain, and shift-work/circadian disruption.

Checks for red flags that would change management (e.g., suicidality, severe mood symptoms, symptoms suggestive of sleep apnea such as loud snoring/witnessed apneas, parasomnias) and indicates escalation/referral if present.
Explains first-line management including CBT-I (stimulus control, sleep restriction, sleep hygiene) and sets expectations on timeline and benefits.
When discussing medication options, includes doxepin as a potential treatment option and describes it as typically low-dose for sleep maintenance, while emphasizing CBT-I as first-line.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), contraindications relevant to insomnia medications (critical; e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding status when applicable, glaucoma/urinary retention for anticholinergic risk, severe hepatic disease, history of mania with antidepressants), and at least briefly considers drug interactions (non-critical).
Avoids unsafe prescribing statements (e.g., endorsing alcohol with sedatives, recommending chronic benzodiazepines as first-line) and advises against driving/operating machinery if sedated.
Provides a follow-up timeframe and monitoring plan in natural language (even if not using a follow-up tool), including reassessment of sleep and adverse effects if medication is used.
